g.data-narration: Before blockchain, digital transactions required trusted third parties like banks to prevent fraud and ensure trust.
g.text: The Problem: Trust in Digital World
Traditional digital transactions need intermediaries (banks) to verify and prevent
double-spending. This creates: single point of failure, high fees, and inefficiency.

g.data-narration: The Byzantine Generals Problem shows how hard it is for decentralized nodes to agree without a trusted central authority.
g.text: Multiple generals must agree to attack/retreat. Some may be traitors sending conflicting messages.
Blockchain solves this via consensus mechanisms enabling untrusted nodes to agree on ledger state.
Proof of Work / Proof of Stake

g.data-narration: The CAP theorem shows blockchains prioritize Availability and Partition Tolerance, sacrificing strong consistency for eventual consistency.
g.text: Distributed systems can only guarantee 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance.
Network partitions are inevitable → P is non-negotiable. Must choose between C and A.

g.data-narration: A blockchain is a decentralized, distributed digital ledger that records transactions across many computers, making records tamper-evident.
g.text: A decentralized, distributed digital ledger used to record transactions across many computers.
Once recorded, data cannot be altered retroactively without changing all subsequent blocks.

g.data-narration: Each block contains a header with metadata (previous hash, nonce, merkle root) and a body with transaction data.
Blockchain transaction flow: initiation → propagation → block creation → consensus → propagation → validation → immutability.
g.text: Altering a transaction requires re-mining ALL subsequent blocks.
Computationally infeasible on large networks = permanent record.

g.data-narration: The hash chain creates immutability: changing any data in a block changes its hash, breaking the chain links.
g.text: Change 1 byte → Hash changes → Chain breaks

g.data-narration: Blockchain core features: decentralization, immutability, transparency, security, and consensus-based validation.
Consensus mechanisms like Proof of Work and Proof of Stake ensure all nodes agree on transaction validity without intermediaries.

g.text: • Validators chosen based on cryptocurrency stake
Both ensure network security and agreement without trusted third parties

g.data-narration: Blockchain applications extend beyond cryptocurrency to supply chains, identity, voting, smart contracts, DeFi, and NFTs.
Three tiers: Public (permissionless, like Bitcoin), Private (permissioned, like Hyperledger), and Consortium (federated).
